rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order For Terminating Sanctions And Striking Claim (15-129)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 21, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA's Motion For Order For Terminating Sanctions And Striking Claim (15-129) Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Petitioner's unopposed motion for terminating sanctions striking the claim of Qiong Wang is granted. If the tentative ruling is not contested, no appearance is required and the Court will sign the proposed order submitted with the motion, but striking the final paragraph (which provides that Petitioner "be allowed" to withdraw the Petition for Forfeiture in Rem and proceed with non-judicial forfeiture proceedings), because the motion papers do not explain the grounds for seeking such relief (or why it is necessary). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: EARNESTINE MAY-MCNARY VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCSICO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Amended Petition Of Writ Of Mandate *****Filed At Insistence Of Litigant*****
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, November 21, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER EARNESTINE MAY-MCNARY's Motion For Amended Petition Of Writ Of Mandate *****Filed At Insistence Of Litigant***** Petitioner Earnestine May-McNary's petition for writ of mandate is denied. The court employs the independent judgment rule when analyzing petitioner's writ of mandate because she has a fundamental vested right to the disability benefits if in fact she was disabled in 2004 when she stopped working for respondent. (See Beckley v. Board of Administration (2013) 222 Cal.App.4th 691, 697.) "[I]n exercising its independent judgment, a trial court must afford a strong presumption of correctness concerning the administrative findings, and the party challenging the administrative decision bears the burden of convincing the court that the administrative findings are contrary to the weight of the evidence." (Fukuda v. City of Angels (1999) 20 Cal.4th 805, 817.) SF Charter § A8.587-3 provides in pertinent part that "[a]ny member who becomes incapacitated for performance of duty because of disability determined by a qualified hearing officer to be of extended and uncertain duration" is entitled to disability benefits. "[T]o be 'incapacitated for the performance of duty'…means the substantial inability of the applicant to perform his usual duties." (Mansperger v. Public Employees Retirement System (1970) 6 Cal.App.3d 873, 876.) In this case, petitioner fails to show that Administrative Law Judge Owyang's decision to deny petitioner's second application for disability benefits is contrary to the weight of the evidence. The record demonstrates that no physician advised petitioner to stop working in 2004 and she was first told to do so in 2010. (AR1 208:24-25; 209:1-25; 210:1-6.) The medical evidence supports the finding that petitioner was not incapacitated for the performance of her duty as a medical transcriber in 2004. (AR2 124-138 (Dr. Talmage's report, pg. 14); AR2 87-110 (Dr. Perl's report, pgs. 23-24.) Lastly, and most importantly, petitioner admitted that she worked in her customary position as a medical transcriber for Sutter Health from 2008-2010. (AR2 280:10-24; 282:14-16.) This evidence strongly suggests that petitioner could perform her regular duties in 2004. Petitioner's credibility was also lacking because when she first applied for benefits, she denied taking any post-retirement employment. (AR1 192:6-11; 211:9-11.) The administrative law judge noted this omission and could reasonably conclude that all or some of petitioner's testimony lacked credibility. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for Respondent is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STACEY CURTIS VS. HOMEBRIDGE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, November 21, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT HOMEBRIDGE, INC.'s DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants' demurrer to the third amended complaint is sustained in its entirety with leave to amend. In her late-filed opposition to Defendants' demurrer and motion to strike and accompanying declaration, Plaintiff does not meaningfully respond to the demurrer, but seeks leave to amend the third amended complaint, and asserts that she needs time to consult an attorney in order to do so. Plaintiff is granted 20 days leave, until December 11, 2019, to file and serve a further amended complaint; no further extensions will be granted. If Plaintiff fails to timely file a fourth amended complaint, Defendants may appear ex parte to seek an order of dismissal with prejudice and entry of judgment. (Code Civ. Proc., § 581(f)(2); Cal. R. Ct. 3.1320(h).) Plaintiff is admonished that this is her last opportunity. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 430.41(e)(1) ["In response to a demurrer and prior to the case being at issue, a complaint . . . shall not be amended more than three times, absent an offer to the trial court as to such additional facts to be pleaded that there is a reasonable possibility the defect can be cured to state a cause of action"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

Ct. 3.1320(h).) Plaintiff is admonished that this is her last opportunity. (See Code Civ. rulings.Case Title: ROUHEL COTRELL VS. DOUGLAS PARKING LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, November 21, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT DOUGLAS PARKING LLC's MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Douglas Parking LLC's motion to strike claim for punitive damages alleged in the First Amended Complaint is granted without leave to amend. In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code § 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) This is a slip and fall action in which Plaintiff charges Defendant with negligence for failing to inspect, maintain, and clean the garage floor as agreed upon in a contract with the garage owner. Plaintiff further alleges Defendants misrepresented they were cleaning the garage for years, when in fact, they were not. Such conduct does not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1). At most, Plaintiff's allegations show "mere carelessness or ignorance, which is insufficient to establish malice." (Pacific Gas and Electric Company, 24 Cal.App.5th at 1171; see Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-1210]; see also Brousseau v. Jarrett (1977) 73 Cal.App.3d 864, 872 ["conclusory characterization of defendant's conduct as intentional, willful and fraudulent is a patently insufficient statement of 'oppression, fraud, or malice, express or implied,' within the meaning of section 3294."].) Contrary to Plaintiff's argument, Defendant's alleged repeated conduct does not give rise to a claim for punitive damages. (Cf. Mazik v. Geico General Ins. Co. (2019) 35 Cal.App.5th 455, 473 [insurance bad faith action in which there was evidence defendant insurer engaged in repeated oppressive conduct, intentionally manipulated the facts to create a favorable record justifying its offers, etc.].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ROUHEL COTRELL VS. DOUGLAS PARKING LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, November 21, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT DOUGLAS PARKING LLC's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Douglass Parking LLC's demurrer is overruled. Defendant's argument that the first cause of action is uncertain and does not state sufficient facts to constitute a cause of action for negligence is groundless. (Code Civ. Proc., § 430.10(e), (f).) A "demurrer for uncertainty is strictly construed, even where a complaint is in some respects uncertain, because ambiguities can be clarified under modern discovery procedures." (Khoury v. Maly's of California (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 616; see also Lickiss v. Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 1125, 1135 ["demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored, and are granted only if the pleading is so incomprehensible that a defendant cannot reasonably respond."].) Plaintiff sufficiently pleads all the elements necessary for a general negligence claim. (See Berkley v. Dowds (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 518, 526 [the elements for a cause of action of negligence are: duty, breach of duty, causation and damages].) The first amended complaint alleges Douglas Parking LLC and David Douglas entered into a contract with the owners of the garage to regularly inspect the garage as well as provide cleaning and power washing to the garage floors. (FAC 8-10, 16.) Plaintiff alleges Defendants did not fulfil their obligations under the contract thereby breaching their duty. (FAC 12, 13, 18, 21.) Specifically, the first amended complaint alleges Defendant David Douglass never told his employees to inspect or wash the floor and that he had no intention of following through with the contract he entered into on behalf of the LLC. (FAC 9, 10, 12.) As such, Defendant Douglas may be held personally liable. (See Cal. Corp. Code, § 16306.) The complaint alleges that Defendants had actual and constructive notice of the alleged dangerous oil slick. (FAC 21; see also Ortega v. Kmart Corp. (2001) 26 Cal.4th 1200, 1206 [actual or constructive knowledge of the dangerous condition is a key to establishing its liability].) Lastly, Plaintiff properly pleads causation and damages. (FAC 14, 20, 23-26.) The first amended complaint states facts sufficient to constitute a general negligence cause of action, pleading both general and special damages. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS WHEELER VS. HAMLINHOTEL L.P ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Contest The Good Faith Of Defendant Hamlin Hotel, L.P.S Application For Good Faith Settlement Determination; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, November 21, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT CARITAS MANAGEMENT CORPORATION's Motion To Contest The Good Faith Of Defendant Hamlin Hotel, L.P.S Application For Good Faith Settlement Determination. Caritas Management Corporation's Motion to Contest Good Faith Settlement is granted. This ruling is without prejudice to settlors renewing their application for a good faith determination upon a proper evidentiary showing. Although settlors assert this is a "miniscule" liability case in light of Caritas' role in hiring and supervising Mr. Bursey, they have not presented any admissible evidence that would enable the Court to determine "a rough approximation of plaintiffs' total recovery and the settlor's proportionate liability." (Tech-Bilt, Inc. v., Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499.) "If 'there is no substantial evidence to support a critical assumption as to the nature and extent of a settling defendant's liability, then a determination of good faith based upon such assumption is an abuse of discretion.'" (Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1348 [settling parties did not proffer any evidence regarding their proportionate liability for plaintiff's alleged injury, but merely presented a series of "questionable assumptions" in their memorandum to show the settlement amount was reasonable].) Even if counsel had personal knowledge of such information, counsel's declaration does not provide any such evidence. (See Greshko v. County of Los Angeles (1987) 194 Cal.App.3d 822, 834 [attorney's declaration regarding settling defendant's liability insufficient where he failed to provide specific supporting facts or expert opinion].) While the party asserting the lack of good faith has the burden on that issue (Code Civ. Proc. § 877.6(d)), the settlors must provide the court in the first instance with a sufficient evidentiary basis upon which to make a good faith determination. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 877.6(b) ["The issue of the good faith of a settlement may be determined on the basis of affidavits served with the notice of hearing, and any counteraffidavits filed in response . . . ."]; Mattco Forge, 38 Cal.App.4th at 1350, n.6.) Settlors have not done so. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Caritas is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DENTAL FORCE, INC. ET AL VS. BEMDAD BEHNOOD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Dental Forces Notice Of Motion And Motion To File Under Seal Reply Brief Submitted In Support Of Dental Forces Motion For Summary Adjudication; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, November 21, 2019, Line 10, PLAINTIFF DENTAL FORCE, INC., EVANGELISTA DENTAL GROUP SAN FRANCISCO 450 SUTTER's Motion To File Under Seal Reply Brief Submitted In Support Of Dental Forces Motion For Summary Adjudication. Grant. No opposition filed, and the motion complies with California Rules of Court, Rules 2.550 and 2.551. The Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. rulings.Case Title: VELOCITY INVESTMENTS, LLC VS. THERESA BRESKI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, November 21, 2019, Line 14, DEFENDANT THERESA BRESKI's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant Theresa Breski's demurrer to the second cause of action of the complaint is overruled. The complaint adequately pleads each element of an open book account. "The only essential allegations of a common count are '(1) the statement of indebtedness in a certain sum, (2) the consideration, i.e., goods sold, work done, etc., and (3) nonpayment.'" (Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Zerin (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 445, 460; see also Interstate Group Administrators, Inc. v. Cravens, Dargan & Co. (1985) 174 Cal.App.3d 700, 708 ["A book account may furnish the basis for an action on a common account ' . . . when it contains a statement of the debits and credits of the transactions involved completely enough to supply evidence from which it can be reasonably determined what amount is due to the claimant.' [Citations.] A book account is described as 'open' when the debtor has made some payment on the account, leaving a balance due."].) "In California, it has long been settled the allegation of claims using common counts is good against special or general demurrers." (Farmers Ins. Exchange, 53 Cal.App.4th at 460.) Defendant's argument that Plaintiff cannot assert such a claim because it has also brought a claim for breach of express contract is wrong. "'It makes no difference in such a case that the proof shows the original transaction to be an express contract, a contract implied in fact, or a quasi-contract.'" (Utility Audit Co., Inc. v. City of Los Angeles (2003) 112 Cal.App.4th 950, 958.) In any event, a party is permitted to plead in the alternative. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FLEXIBLE FUNDING LTD. LIABILITY CO. VS. TILLEY HVR, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Change Venue
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, November 21, 2019, Line 15, DEFENDANT TILLEY HVR, LLC's Motion To Change Venue. Defendant Tilley HVR, LLC's motion to change venue is denied. On a motion to change venue, the burden is on the moving party to show (1) that venue is proper in a different county than the one in which the lawsuit was filed and (2) that venue is improper in the current county under any applicable theory. (La Mirada Comm. Hosp. v. Sup. Ct. (1967) 249 Cal.App.2d 39, 42.) Defendant fails to show that venue is improper in San Francisco County under any applicable theory. Under Code of Civil Procedure § 395.5, a "corporation or association may be sued in the county where the contract is made or is to be performed, or where the obligation or liability arises." Here, venue is proper in San Francisco because Defendant was allegedly obligated to send payment of its invoices to Plaintiff in San Francisco. (See Mission Imports, Inc. v. Superior Court (1982) 31 Cal.3d 921, 928-929 ["Section 395.5 permits tort claims against a corporation to be tried in any county in which the 'liability arises' as well as in the county where the corporation's principal place of business is located."]; Anaheim Extrusion Co. v. Superior Court (1985) 170 Cal.App.3d 1201, 1203 [where corporation's breach is failure to pay for goods sold and delivered, the "place of performance" is the place where payments were to be made].) Defendant allegedly became liable to Plaintiff when Plaintiff did not receive the money owed to it at the San Francisco address provided in the Notices of Assignment and the invoices as required under Commercial Code section 9406. (Deluca Decl., Exs. B, C, D.) Although it is contested whether Defendant was made aware of such obligation by the March 2017 Notice of Assignment, the June 2018 Amended Notice of Assignment also provides for payment in San Francisco. Thus, Defendant has failed to rebut the presumption that San Francisco is a proper venue. (See Tokuzo Shida v. Japan Food Corp. (1960) 185 Cal.App.2d 443, 448 [defendant failed to show facts to "controvert the presumption in favor of plaintiff that the liability for the alleged tort of inducing breach of contract arose" in the plaintiff's chosen forum].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)
